heading: MODULO NONO
card_summary: This chunk opens the "Nozze" (Nuptials) section of the Cantico Terzo, treating the mystical marriage between God and the soul as the culminating sacramental structure of Zorzi's cosmological edifice. Zorzi argues, drawing on Genesis 2 (the rib of Adam), Paul's letter to the Ephesians, Hosea, Ezekiel, the Book of Revelation, Isaiah, and the Song of Songs, that the divine-human marriage was instituted at creation, contracted in baptism, ratified in confirmation, and will be consummated in the eschatological union of Christ and the Church. The Hebrew term rachamim (compassionate loves) is parsed to distinguish the juridical covenant of the Mosaic law from the merciful covenant of Christ, and the Church is typologically identified as the "woman" drawn from Christ's side as Eve was drawn from Adam's rib. Augustine's commentary on the sacraments flowing from Christ's pierced side is explicitly invoked, and Paul's dictum "qui adhaeret Deo unus spiritus efficitur" frames the consummation as pneumatic unification. The fourfold structure — initiation, contraction, ratification, consummation — maps precisely onto the Scholastic theology of matrimony, transposed onto a Christological-anagogical plane.
essay:
## Overview: The Mystical Marriage as Structural Climax

Chunk 118 inaugurates the "Nozze" (Nuptials) section within the Cantico Terzo. Zorzi presents the marriage between God and the soul not as a mere devotional metaphor but as the literal structural apex of creation: the world-edifice was built precisely in order to host this wedding. The argument proceeds through a rigorous fourfold theological schema borrowed from Scholastic matrimonial theology — *initiatum*, *contractum*, *ratificatum*, *consummatum* — and maps each stage onto sacramental events in the life of the Christian soul.

## 1. The Institution of the Marriage (Concentus I)

Zorzi opens by citing the creation narrative of Genesis 2: God fashioned Eve from Adam's rib and brought her to him as wife, so that the human race could experience the "symmetry of its like through the application of active to passive." This establishes the foundational typology: Eve from Adam's rib prefigures the Church drawn from Christ's side.

Paul's gloss on Genesis 2:24 in Ephesians 5 — "questo mistero è grande, lo dico in riferimento a Cristo e alla chiesa" — is treated as the hermeneutic key that authorizes the entire allegorical program. The inclusio of Scripture is noted: the Bible opens with a wedding (Genesis) and closes with one (Apocalypse), when the blessed cry out:

> Rallegriamoci ed esultiamo perché sono giunte le nozze dell'Agnello; e ancora: Beati quelli che sono invitati alla cena delle nozze dell'Agnello.

<cite>p. 2593–2594</cite>

The identity of the Bridegroom is established through Hosea 2 ("Ti sposerò nella fedeltà") and the parable of the king's son in Matthew 22. Zorzi then offers a crucial kabbalistic-linguistic aside on the Hebrew of Hosea 2:21–22: the word *rachamim* (rendered in the Latin Vulgate and Italian as "misericordie/compassioni") is parsed as meaning *benigni amori* or *pietà*. This allows Zorzi to distinguish two covenantal registers: the Mosaic law, in which God espoused Israel "nel giudizio e nella giustizia" (the severe spouse), and the messianic covenant, in which Christ espouses the soul "nella misericordia, pietà, grazia" — the fullness of *rachamim*.

> In lege enim severus sponsus desponsavit in iudicio, et iustitia, sed in Messiah praebens se benignissimum desponsavit in misericordia, pietate, gratia, et omnibus benedictionibus per illud verbum rahamim significatis.

<cite>p. 2427 (Latin column)</cite>

The universality of Christ as Bridegroom is defended against an implied objection — how can one Bridegroom suffice for countless souls? — through a Neoplatonic analogy: his riches "non diminuiscono se le si elargisce," like the light of the sun which fecundates all things without diminishing. This is a direct application of the Plotinian doctrine of the inexhaustible overflow of the One, here given a matrimonial-sacramental form.

## 2. The Contraction of the Marriage in Baptism (Concentus II)

Zorzi maps the formal contraction of the mystical marriage onto the sacrament of baptism. The distinction between *verba de praesenti* (present-tense consent, which creates marriage immediately) and *verba de futuro* (betrothal for minors, which can be dissolved) structures the argument: the prophetic invitations of the Old Testament were betrothal; baptism is the real present-tense marriage contract.

The joining of right hands in the marriage ceremony is represented by immersion in water, and the matrimonial formula "Volo te in meam / Volo te in meum" finds its baptismal counterpart in the renunciation: "Rinuncio al diavolo, al mondo e ai suoi splendori per aderire pienamente al mio sposo Cristo."

Ezekiel 16:8–9 ("Ti feci un giuramento, ho stabilito un patto con te... ti lavai con acqua, ti mondai dal tuo sangue e ti unsi con l'olio") is read as a prophetic type of baptism, spoken to the soul-as-Jerusalem, with the washing in water, the cleansing from blood, and the anointing with oil corresponding exactly to the baptismal rites.

The Eve/Church typology is then completed via Augustine:

> Quella de costa viri, et haec de latere Christi, a quo (inquit Augustinus) omnia sacramenta emanarunt, maxime baptismus, qui habuit efficaciam ab illo sanguine, et aqua.

<cite>p. 2432 (Latin column)</cite>

As Eve was formed from Adam's sleeping side, the Church is formed from Christ's side pierced on the cross. The consummation of this unity is framed in Pauline pneumatology: "Qui adhaeret Deo unus spiritus efficitur cum eo" (1 Corinthians 6:17), the soul-bride and Christ-Bridegroom become "un solo spirito."

Zorzi also addresses the question of divorce and repudiation. Christ will never repudiate the Church universal, even though individual souls can commit "adultery" (sin) and be expelled. The figure of Vashti (Esther 1), who was repudiated for scorning the royal command, typifies the Synagogue, which was "divorced" because of ingratitude. The Church-as-new-bride takes her place, with the number of the elect kept perpetually full by the replacement of expelled members: "Dio schiaccerà molti, e farà stare altri al loro posto" (Job) and "Tieni stretto ciò che hai, perché nessuno prenda la tua corona" (Apocalypse 3:11).

## 3. The Ratification in Confirmation (Concentus III)

Confirmation (*confirmatio*) is read as the ratification of the marriage bond before the bishop (analogously to ratification before a priest in civil matrimony). The sacrament of chrism (*chrisma*) is glossed as the "particular anointing of divinity."

A significant Hebrew analysis is inserted here: Psalm 51:14 in the Vulgate reads "spiritu principali confirma me," but Zorzi notes the Hebrew term is *nediv* (נְדִיב), meaning "willing, generous, liberal, munificent" — not simply "steadfast." This correction of the Latin rendering is characteristic of Zorzi's Hebraist approach to the Psalms.

The locus of final ratification is identified as the heavenly Jerusalem, where Christ confirmed the marriage covenant on the cross and through the infusion of the Holy Spirit. Jerome is cited on the necessity of heavenly confirmation given human weakness: "conferma i nostri matrimoni dall'alto (dice Gerolamo) perché siamo deboli."

## 4. The Consummation in Good Works and Eschatological Union (Concentus IV)

The consummation of the spiritual marriage occurs when the soul carries out in good works what it promised in baptism. Isaiah 26:17–18 — "abbiamo concepito dal tuo volto, Signore... e abbiamo partorito lo spirito" — provides the scriptural figure for this spiritual pregnancy and birth, where the soul conceives from the face of God as a wife conceives from the face of her husband.

The eschatological terminus of the whole argument is the entry into the eternal "tende e talami" (tabernacles and bridal chambers), where the union with the Bridegroom will be "maggiore e più perfetta e... in perpetuo." The HymeneusSpirit (*il santissimo imeneo che è lo spirito e il legame di Dio*) binds and unites all those it pervades.

The Song of Songs provides the vocabulary for this consummation:

> Allora si scambieranno gli autentici baci, dei quali nel Cantico si dice: Mi baci con il bacio della sua bocca. Allora ci abbandoneremo agli abbracci castissimi e santissimi dell'amore, dei quali nel medesimo Cantico si dice: La sua sinistra sarà sotto il mio capo e la sua destra mi abbraccerà.

<cite>p. 2435</cite>

The section closes with Christ's high-priestly prayer from John 17 ("ut omnes unum sint, sicuti tu Pater in me, et ego in te"), which Zorzi reads as the Bridegroom's prayer for the ultimate pneumatic fusion of all souls with the Trinity — the final consummation toward which "tutti quanti tendiamo."

## Connections to Zorzi's Larger Project

This chunk is the explicit sacramental-nuptial culmination of the entire *De Harmonia Mundi*. The fourfold marriage schema (initiation/contraction/ratification/consummation) mirrors Zorzi's habitual use of quaternary harmonic structures throughout the work. The Neoplatonic motif of inexhaustible divine light fecundating all things without diminishing connects the matrimonial theology to the emanationist cosmology of the earlier cantici. The kabbalistic readings of *rachamim* and *nediv* demonstrate that Zorzi's Christian Kabbalah is still operative even in this climactic, ecclesiological section — Hebrew textual analysis is not ornamental but hermeneutically load-bearing, revealing the deeper covenantal grammar that Latin translation conceals.
